text:
According to the mechanisms involved in extractive hydrometallurgy, commercial solvent extractants may be classified according to their ability to transport the metal species from the aqueous phase into the water-immiscible organic phase as metal cations ($M^{n+}$), as metalate anions ($MX_x^{n-}$) or as neutral metal salts ($MX_x$). A very efficient and selective extractive action can be based on adequate coordination chemistry principles.

Complexations in the inner and outer coordination sphere determine the selectivity of the extractants for the metal cation or the metal salt. Specific donor atoms and conformations are combined in exceptionally stable neutral complexes, soluble in the hydrocarbon diluents employed in commercial hydrotrope-based processes. Although much less defined, the structures formed in metalate extractions are in general composed of a higher degree of flexibility. In the organic phase, most cationic reagents interact with the metal of the metalate in the outer coordination sphere, the secondary bonds (electrostatic, hydrogen-bond type) being greatly enhanced in the low-polarity organic phase. This chapter aims at illustrating how insight in inner- and outer-sphere complexations is of valuable assistance in the design of improved process for metals recoveries.
